claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 3 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 8 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 10 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 18 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 3 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit! Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to know a bit about your child (or children). 

**How old is your child?**

This is important because:
- Children **under 16** automatically qualify (subject to other conditions)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** typically don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age (or if you have multiple children, let me know all of their ages).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I have three children actually - Alex is 3, Blake is 8, and Charlie is 10. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! You have three children:
- **Alex** (age 3)
- **Blake** (age 8)
- **Charlie** (age 10)

All three of your children are under 16, which is great - they fall into the automatic qualification age category. Now I need to check a few more things for each child.

Let me start with the next question:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Are any of your children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie) working 24 hours or more per week in paid employment?**

Given their young ages (3, 8, and 10), I expect the answer is no, but I need to confirm this to continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of them work. They're all too young for that - they're just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that makes sense! Since they're all in primary school and not working, we can move forward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Are any of your children receiving benefits in their own right?**

This would include things like:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Income Support

These are benefits that would be claimed by the child themselves (not benefits you receive for them). Given their ages, this is unlikely, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of them receive any benefits in their own names. They're just kids.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's what I expected given their ages.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Are any of your children enrolled in an apprenticeship program?**

Again, given that Alex (3), Blake (8), and Charlie (10) are all in primary school, I expect the answer is no, but I need to confirm this.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not in any apprenticeships - they're far too young for that. They're just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's correct - they're definitely too young for apprenticeships! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status:

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

You'll meet the residency requirement if any of the following apply to you:
- You normally live in the UK
- You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
- You have pre-settled status and meet certain conditions (working, studying, sufficient resources, etc.)
- You're a Crown servant living abroad

Do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Living in Manchester means you meet the residency requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Are you responsible for Alex, Blake, and Charlie?**

You're considered responsible if any of the following apply:
- You live with the children
- You contribute to their upkeep (paying at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards their care - things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing)
- You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Do you live with your three children?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex and Charlie live with me at home. Blake doesn't live with me though - he lives with his mum.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. So:
- **Alex** and **Charlie** live with you
- **Blake** lives with his mum

Since you live with Alex and Charlie, you meet the responsibility requirement for them.

For **Blake**, even though he doesn't live with you, you could still be considered responsible if you contribute to his upkeep. 

**Do you contribute financially to Blake's care?** 

This could include:
- Regular payments for his food, clothes, or other needs
- Maintenance payments under a court order or agreement
- Other financial contributions (at least equivalent to the Child Benefit amount, which would be around £25 per week for one child)

Do you make any financial contributions towards Blake's upkeep?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not currently contributing financially to Blake's care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That means:
- **Alex** and **Charlie**: You meet the responsibility requirement (you live with them)
- **Blake**: You don't meet the responsibility requirement (doesn't live with you and you don't contribute to his upkeep)

Let me continue checking the eligibility for Alex and Charlie, who you are responsible for.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Charlie?**

This is important because only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time. For example:
- Is their other parent claiming for them?
- Is another family member or guardian claiming for them?

Are Alex and Charlie currently being claimed for by anyone else?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's clear for Alex and Charlie. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check the living situation for Alex and Charlie:

**Are either Alex or Charlie currently:**
- In hospital?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?
- Or are they living normally at home with you?

What's their current living situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex and Charlie are living normally at home with me. They're not in hospital, care, or being fostered or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! They're living normally at home with you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
One final question about Alex and Charlie:

**Is the local council paying towards their accommodation or maintenance costs?**

This would mean the council is financially supporting their living costs. Given they live with you at home, I expect the answer is no, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards their costs. I take care of them myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment Results**

### **Summary:**
✅ You **ARE eligible** to claim Child Benefit for **Alex** and **Charlie**  
❌ You **ARE NOT eligible** to claim Child Benefit for **Blake**

---

### **Individual Assessment:**

**✅ Alex (age 3) - ELIGIBLE**
- Under 16 years old ✓
- Lives with you ✓
- Not working ✓
- Not receiving benefits in their own right ✓
- Not in an apprenticeship ✓
- No one else is claiming for them ✓
- Living normally at home ✓
- Local council not paying for their care ✓

**❌ Blake (age 8) - NOT ELIGIBLE for your claim**
- Although Blake meets the age criteria (under 16), you **do not meet the responsibility requirement** for him
- Blake lives with his mum, not with you
- You are not contributing financially to his care
- **Blake's mother would be the appropriate person to claim Child Benefit for him** (if she meets all the eligibility requirements)

**✅ Charlie (age 10) - ELIGIBLE**
- Under 16 years old ✓
- Lives with you ✓
- Not working ✓
- Not receiving benefits in their own right ✓
- Not in an apprenticeship ✓
- No one else is claiming for them ✓
- Living normally at home ✓
- Local council not paying for their care ✓

---

### **Next Steps for Alex and Charlie:**

1. **Apply for Child Benefit** - You can apply online or by post using form CH2
2. **Prepare documentation:**
   - Proof of your identity
   - Birth certificates or adoption certificates for Alex and Charlie
3. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge:** If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay a tax charge
4. **Payments:** Child Benefit is usually paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

**Apply here:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

---

Do you have any questions about this assessment or the next steps?

reasonings: Alex (age 3) is eligible for Child Benefit. Alex is under 16 years old, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with you (meeting the responsibility requirement), is not being claimed for by anyone else, is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake (age 8) is not eligible for your claim. Although Blake is under 16 years old and meets the age qualification criteria, you do not meet the responsibility requirement for Blake. Blake does not live with you (lives with his mum), and you confirmed that you are not contributing financially to Blake's care. Without meeting the responsibility requirement through either living with the child or contributing to their upkeep, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie (age 10) is eligible for Child Benefit. Charlie is under 16 years old, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with you (meeting the responsibility requirement), is not being claimed for by anyone else, is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You can claim Child Benefit for 2 out of your 3 children. Alex and Charlie both meet all eligibility requirements as they live with you and satisfy all other criteria. However, you cannot claim for Blake because you do not meet the responsibility requirement - Blake lives with his mum and you are not contributing financially to his care. Blake's mother would be the appropriate person to claim Child Benefit for him if she meets the eligibility requirements.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)
